title: The To-Do List Parked Outside the Bedroom Door
subtitle: A visualisation for separating work-mode from sleep-mode
excerpt: A five-minute visualisation for Day 7 of the sleep arc. The image places the day's unfinished business outside the bedroom — visible, not lost, but given a clear physical location that is outside the space where sleep happens.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. Built from the Day 7 theme of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025, ISBN 9798269649795): unhooking from busyness at bedtime.

practice.whenToUse: When the to-do list is running as a background loop at bedtime — use this lying down in bed.

content.text: A short visualisation for Day 7 of the sleep arc. Use it lying down in bed. The image gives the to-do list a physical location outside the bedroom — so it isn't gone, but it isn't here.
Picture a clipboard. It holds everything still on the list — every task, every email, every unresolved thing from today. The list is real; it's all there.
Now picture getting up and placing the clipboard just outside your bedroom door. Put it down on the floor. It's not lost — it's right there, six feet away, waiting for morning.
Close the door. You can hear the corridor beyond it, but the clipboard stays on the other side.
Notice the bedroom without the list in it. The room is the same. The bed is there. Nothing has changed except the list isn't in the room with you.
If the mind reaches for the list again, picture opening the door. Glance at the clipboard: it's still there, nothing has been lost. Close the door again.
Stay in the room. The morning will bring the clipboard back.
Original to homemade.education. Built from the Day 7 theme of
(Rebecca J Page, 2025): unhooking from busyness at bedtime. Externalisation of worry as a sleep-hygiene technique has been recommended in CBT-I (Cognitive Behavioural Therapy for Insomnia) contexts.
